coredata.dc:title: FRET as a novel strategy to enhance the singlet oxygen generation of porphyrinic MOF decorated self-disinfecting fabrics
coredata.dc:creator: Nie, Xiaolin
Wu, Shuanglin
Mensah, Alfred
Wang, Qingqing
Huang, Fenglin
Wei, Qufu
coredata.dc:description: Self-disinfecting textiles with high efficient antimicrobial function play a vital role in the prevention of pathogen transmissions, such as bacteria in hospitals and medical institutions. However, low reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation of current photodynamic materials cannot meet the efficiency of microbial inactivation. Herein, to increase the singlet oxygen (1O2) generation, we constructed a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) pair with GQDs as the donor and PCN-224 as the acceptor. The GQDs and PCN-224 were immobilized onto the cotton fiber surface by chemical coupling and in-situ growth, respectively. A series of properties including the promotion effect of 1O2 and the photodynamic antibacterial effect of the self-disinfecting fabrics against Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria were studied, the results showed that 1O2 generation was increased by 1.61-fold via FRET process. The fabrics showed high bactericidal efficiency (99.9999+% against four kinds of bacterial strains in 30 min) and low cytotoxicity. The construction of self-disinfecting textiles based on the photodynamic and FRET strategy proposed in this work is of constructive significance for expanding and promoting the applications of textile materials in medical fields.

Photographic and SEM images of KCF (Panel A, B, and C) and PCN-224/KCF-GQDs (Panel D, E, and F). Panel G is the enlarged view of PCN-224/KCF-GQDs. Panel H is the size distribution of PCN-224 on cotton fiber from Panel G.
SEM image, EDS results and EDS elemental distribution images identifying the element of C, N, O, and Zr.

Panel A: FT-IR spectra of KCF, KCF-NH2, KCF-GQDs, and GQDs. Panel B: XRD pattern of KCF-GQDs, and PCN-224/KCF-GQDs. The inset in panel B is the enlarged XRD pattern of PCN-224/KCF-GQDs, 2θ from 4 °to 12°.

Panel A: UV–visible absorption spectra of GQDs and PCN-224, and fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs (excitation wavelength: 360 nm). Panel B: fluorescence emission spectra of KCF-GQDs, PCN-224/KCF and PCN-224/KCF-GQDs.

UV–visible spectra of the photooxidation of 18.5 μM DPBF to colorless 1,2-dibenzoylbenzene in the presence of PCN-224/KCF-GQDs (Panel A) and PCN-224/KCF (Panel B). The curves were recorded every two minutes under a handheld laser (532 nm, 85 ± 1 mW/cm2 fluence rate). The photooxidation rate constant of DPBF in the presence of PCN-224/KCF-GQDs and PCN-224/KCF (Panel C). Generation of excited PS states and 1O2 (Panel D).

aPDI studies employing PCN-224/KCF and PCN-224/KCF-GQDs against bacterial strains. Displayed is the % survival for the PCN-224/KCF-GQDs dark control, KCF illumination control, PCN-224/KCF and PCN-224/KCF-GQDs under illumination.
SEM images of E. coli (Panel A, C) and S. aureus (Panel B, D) on PCN-224/KCF-GQDs before (Panel A and B) and after (Panel C and D) inactivation.

head.ce:title: FRET as a novel strategy to enhance the singlet oxygen generation of porphyrinic MOF decorated self-disinfecting fabrics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Eco-Textiles, Ministry of Education, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Eco-Textiles, Ministry of Education, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Key Laboratory of Novel Functional Textile Fibers and Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Fujian Key Laboratory of Novel Functional Textile Fibers and Materials, Minjiang University, Fuzhou, Fujian 350108, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory of Eco-textiles, Jiangnan University, 1800 Lihu Avenue, Wuxi 214122, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Self-disinfecting textiles with high efficient antimicrobial function play a vital role in the prevention of pathogen transmissions, such as bacteria in hospitals and medical institutions. However, low reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation of current photodynamic materials cannot meet the efficiency of microbial inactivation. Herein, to increase the singlet oxygen (1O2) generation, we constructed a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) pair with GQDs as the donor and PCN-224 as the acceptor. The GQDs and PCN-224 were immobilized onto the cotton fiber surface by chemical coupling and in-situ growth, respectively. A series of properties including the promotion effect of 1O2 and the photodynamic antibacterial effect of the self-disinfecting fabrics against Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria were studied, the results showed that 1O2 generation was increased by 1.61-fold via FRET process. The fabrics showed high bactericidal efficiency (99.9999+% against four kinds of bacterial strains in 30 min) and low cytotoxicity. The construction of self-disinfecting textiles based on the photodynamic and FRET strategy proposed in this work is of constructive significance for expanding and promoting the applications of textile materials in medical fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infections posed a serious threat to people all over the world, bringing about significant morbidity and mortality [1,2], commonly misused and overused antibiotics have led to the emergence of drug-resistant bacteria [3]. Therefore, great efforts were made to developing novel and nonspecific antibacterial materials in recent years [4–6]. Among many antibacterial materials, fabrics with self-disinfecting function have received more and more attentions [7,8]. Particularly, inspired by medical textiles for preventing pathogen transmission among the bacterial environment in hospital, researchers put their attention to the functionalization of antibacterial for cotton textiles [9,10]. Cotton textiles are widely used in medical institutions. Because of the porous structure and easy contamination by blood, body fluids, and secretions, cotton fabrics used in hospitals are likely to become reservoirs of pathogens. In other words, cotton textiles provide a breeding ground for the survival of bacteria and can lead to bacteria transmission. In order to solve the mentioned problems, some finishing methods with antibacterial function were employed into cotton textiles, including antibacterial materials coating [11,12], chemical immobilization and dying [9,13]. Even with these methods applied to help solve the bacterial transmission problems, one or more limitations do exist, including complex chemical process, bacterial resistance, inefficiency of bacterial inactivation, bacterial inactivation which is limited to a single species of bacteria and/or unsatisfying side effects for the dressing application.

To overcome some of the above shortcomings, the photodynamic inactivation strategy was incorporated into fabric materials, leading to scalable, nonspecific, and robust textiles [14–16]. Antibacterial photodynamic inactivation (aPDI) process needs three key elements: a harmless photosensitizer (PS), oxygen molecules (O2), and light illumination (usually visible light). A successful aPDI requires the above three factors to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS) through photochemical reactions, including hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), hydroxyl radical (OH), superoxide anion radical (O2
−) (Type I mechanism) and/or singlet oxygen (1O2) (Type II mechanism). ROS could cause serious irreversible damage to bacterial cells, which inactivates bacteria [17,18]. Particularly, aPDI has the following advantageous properties: (i) aPDI could effectively inactivate multiple classes of pathogens [19–21], including bacteria (both drug-resistant and drug-susceptible strains) [22,23], fungi, and virus; (ii) aPDI is unlikely to make pathogens resistant, since the apoptosis caused by ROS is nonspecific and non-triggering [24]. One of the elements to ensure the normal process of aPDI is the suitable PS selection. Current PSs used in aPDI, including natural compounds (e.g., curcumin [25] and hypericin [26]), synthetic dyes (e.g., methylene blue [27] and rose Bengal [28]), tetrapyrroles (i.e., porphyrins [29], chlorins [30], and phthalocyanines [31]) and boron-dipyrromethene (a.k.a. BODIPY [32,33]) are non-scalable (high cost or complex synthesis), limited diffusion of 1O2, and/or lack the desirable photo or physical properties. Thus, novel superior PSs are urgently needed for aPDI.

Recently, nanosized Zr-based porphyrinic metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), PCN-224 nanoparticles, were synthesized for photodynamic therapy (PDT) [34]. As a novel class of crystalline material, PCN-224 was widely used as PS due to the advantages of efficient diffusion of 1O2 (the porous structure) [35], great tenability [36], avoidable self-degradation [37,38], and not susceptible to environment [39,40]. As a new PS, PCN-224 had been widely studied and applied in PDT or aPDI, and achieved good results [41–44]. Although these researches could be regarded as successful strategies, we proposed that the photodynamic efficiency of PCN-224 could be further increased. To address this issue, we employed fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), which could be used as a versatile strategy to enhance ROS generation [29,45–48]. As a non-radiative energy transfer process, FRET is a mechanism that describes the energy transfer between two photosensitive molecules [49–52], and a successful FRET process requires an excited-state donor molecule and a ground-state acceptor molecule [53]. Besides, FRET can only occur when the distance of donor and acceptor is limited to 10 nm, and the absorbance spectra of the acceptor overlaps with the emission of the donor [54]. In order to enhance the aPDI efficiency as being inspired by the FRET strategy, we designed a novel knitted cotton fabric with enhanced 1O2 generation via FRET process. So far, there is no report on textile materials with FRET process and its application for aPDI.

Herein, for the first time, we developed a novel textile material with enhanced 1O2 generation and high aPDI efficiency. Briefly, the amino group was introduced into the knitted cotton fabrics through amination, then graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were grafted onto the cotton fiber surface via amide bond. Finally, PCN-224 nanoparticles were in-situ synthesized on the surface of cotton fibers. Our selection of GQDs and PCN-224 nanoparticles were based on the following: (i) GQDs, a new class of zero-dimensional carbonaceous nanomaterials, have been used in a number of applications [55,56]. As a donor of the FRET pair, the GQDs used in this research have strong fluorescence emission peak between 400 and 600 nm; (ii) PCN-224 nanoparticles, a porphyrinic MOFs, is a crystalline material that produces 1O2, and its porous structure is conducive to the diffusion of 1O2. Furthermore, as an acceptor of the FRET pair, PCN-224 processes strong visible light absorption in the 400–500 nm region, which overlaps with the emission spectra of GQDs to run FRET process. In addition to the characterizations by physical (TEM, SEM, EDS, XRD, tensile strength, TGA, contact angle, zeta potential), spectroscopic (FT-IR, UV–visible, fluorescence), and MTT study with L929 cells, we evaluated the 1O2 generation difference and the ability of the bactericidal textile materials against both Gram-negative (Escherichia coli ATCC-8099, Pseudomonas aeruginosa CMCC (B) 10104) and Gram-positive (Staphylococcus aureus ATCC-6538, Bacillus subtilis CMCC (B) 63501) bacteria. Taken together, the results indicatedthe feasibility of increasing the 1O2 generation and high efficient self-disinfecting textile materials for the prevention of pathogen infection.

Knitted cotton fabrics (KCF, 240 g/m2, 32 s) were provided bythe Engineering Research Center for Knitting Technology at Jiangnan University. Graphene quantum dots(GQDs, XF152) were obtained from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd. 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) and 3-(2-Aminoethylamino)propyl-dimethoxymethylsilane (AEAPMDS) were obtained from Shanghai Vita Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. 5,10,15,20-Tetrakis(4-carboxyphenyl)porphyrin (TCPP), ZrOCl2·8H2O, benzoic acid, 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethyllaminopropyl)carbodiimide (EDC), N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ethanol, N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), glutaric dialdehyde (25%), sodium chloride, yeast extract, potassium chloride, plate count agar, tween-80, butanol, monopotassium phosphate, tryptone, disodium hydrogen phosphate, and soy peptone were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All the chemicals and reagents were of analytical grade and were not purified any further. Deionized water was sterilized at 121 ℃ and used through all the experiments.

AEAPMDS was used as amination agent to achieve aminated knitted cotton fabric (KCF-NH2) by a one-pot water bath method. In order to remove textile auxiliaries before modification, KCF was washed in boiling deionized water for 20 min and then dried in the oven. In brief, a piece of KCF (70 × 50 mm) was placed on the bottom of the beaker, then 12% AEAPMDS was added. The beaker was sealed and stirred at 90 ℃ for 10 h. When the reaction was over and the temperature was cooled down, the KCF was soaked in butanol for 8 h and repeated three times for complete solvent displacement. The KCF-NH2 was finally obtained by freeze-drying.

Before immobilization, the carboxylic acid group of the GQDs was activated by EDC and NHS. Briefly, 40 mL PBS aqueous solution containing 1 mg/mL GQDs, 0.042 M EDC and 0.104 M NHS was shaking at 37 ℃ for 15 min. Then, the KCF-NH2 was added, the mixture was shaken at 37 ℃ overnight. After that, extensive washing of the KCF-GQDs with deionized water (40 mL × 5) followed to remove the unreacted chemical reagents. Finally, the KCF-GQDs was obtained by vacuum-drying at 60 ℃ for 6 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-situ growth of PCN-224 on KCF-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In-situ growth of PCN-224 on KCF-GQDs substrate (PCN-224/KCF-GQDs) was performed by a facile one-pot solvothermal method. Briefly, TCPP (100 mg, 0.126 mmol), benzoic acid (2.8 g, 22.928 mmol) and ZrOCl2·8H2O (300 mg, 0.931 mmol) were dissolved in DMF (100 mL) and stirring for 10 min. After totally dissolving, the mixture was poured into a round-bottom flask containing a piece of KCF-GQDs substrate (70 × 50 mm). The flask was then transferred to an oil bath at 90 ℃ for 5 h. After the reaction was finished and cooled down to room temperature, the substrate was washed by absolute DMF (40 mL × 5) and ethanol (40 mL × 5). The final PCN-224/KCF-GQDs was obtained after vacuum-drying at 60 ℃ for 6 h. The fabrics were preserved in dark.

The morphology and structure of cotton fabrics and fibers were imaged by a scanning electron microscopy (SEM, SU1510). The size and morphology of PCN-224 nanoparticles and GQDs were examined by a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100). The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were measured by a Nicolet Nexus spectrometer (USA). Crystalstructure was analyzed by an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku Ultimate IV, Japan), data ranged from 2θ = 2° to 40° with a speed of 2.0 °/min. Elemental mapping and analysis were performed using an EDAX Octane Elect Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy instrument (EDS, USA). The mechanical tensile strength of the fabrics was obtained using an electronic fabrics strength tester (YG028, China). For the tensile test, the fabric samples (5 × 70 mm) were prepared and a trapezoid area was cut in the middle of samples. 50 mm clamping distance and 20 mm/min stretching speed were set to perform strength study. Thermogravimetric behavior analysis was carried out on a TGA 5500 (USA), and the heating rate was 10 ℃/min with nitrogen flowing. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded by fluoro-spectrophotometer equipped with a Xenon lamp (F-4600, Japan). UV–visible spectra were recorded with a UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu, Japan). The wettability tests were carried out with a contact angle measurement (DCAT-21, Germany). Zeta potential were collected on a Zeta Potential Analyzer (Nano ZS90, UK).

DPBF was used as a quencher to identify the generation of 1O2 produced by PCN-224/KCF-GQDs and PCN-224/KCF. Briefly, a piece of fabric (10 × 10 mm), was added into a 10 mL beaker, then absolute methanol containing 18.5 μM DPBF was added. The beaker was preserved out of the light, until the equilibrium absorption of DPBF on PCN-224. After that, methanol with DPBF in the presence of fabrics were exposed to illumination (532 nm, 85 ± 1 mW/cm2 fluence rate), and the absorbance spectra were obtained every 2 min from 350 nm to 450 nm.

Sterilized lysogeny broth (LB) was used to culture Escherichia coli ATCC-8099 at 37 °C, the same culture condition for Pseudomonas aeruginosa CMCC (B) 10104, Bacillus subtilis CMCC (B) 63501, and Staphylococcus aureus ATCC-6538 but replacing lysogeny broth with tryptic soy broth (TSB). A concentration of about 1–5 × 108 colony forming units (CFU)/mL bacterial strains was confirmed by optical density (OD: 600). Bacteria were centrifuged (8000 rpm, 10 min), then resuspended into PBS solution for the subsequent antibacterial experiments.

Photodynamic antibacterial inactivation assay was performed using a 500 W Xenon lamp, and the vertical distance of illumination was 15 cm, and the wavelength was adjusted through a filter, light with a wavelength less than 420 nm was filtered.

A Sterile 24-well plate was used to perform the inactivation studies of four kinds of bacterial strains. Briefly, three pieces of PCN-224/KCF-GQDs or PCN-224/KCF (10 × 10 mm) were placed on the bottom of three adjacent wells, then 0.1 mL PBS solution containing bacteria was dripped onto each fabric. The 24-well plate was placed in dark for 1 h and then illuminated (500 W, 15 cm, λ ≥ 420 nm, 30 min). All the inactivation experiments were carried out in triplicate, with dark control (bacteria cultured with PCN-224/KCF-GQDs without illumination), KCF illumination control (cotton fabric under illumination), and materials-free dark control.

After illumination, 0.9 mL absolute PBS solution was added into both illuminated and control wells. 0.1 mL aliquots were taken out from each well and diluted 10 times. Continuous dilution of 1:10 was conducted a total of 6 times. Then each of the series was plated on the agar culture plate, TSB culture plate was for S. aureus, B. subtilis, and P. aeruginosa, while LB culture plate was for E. coli. All the plates were incubated away from light at 37 °C, until the bacterial colonies were visible. Because of the limitations by the plating operation, the detection of 6 log units was possible. Survival rate values limited to 0.0001% were recorded, representing the detection limit. The statistical significance was determined by a Student’s two-tailed t-test.

The morphology of the bacteria before and after inactivation was observed by a scanning electron microscopy. After illumination, the fabrics were transferred from the wells into an Eppendorf tube containing 2.5% glutaraldehyde. Then, the Eppendorf tube was incubated at 4 °C for 12 h. After fixing the morphology, the bacteria attached on the cotton fibers were dehydrated by a series of 25%, 50%, 75%, 80%, 95%, and 100% ethanol. The bacteria were freeze-dried and gold-sputtered before visualization by SEM.

MTT assay was performed to evaluate the in vitro cytotoxicity of PCN-224/KCF-GQDs. L929 cells were grown in Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium (DMEM, USA), supplemented with fetal bovine serum (FBS, 10%), penicillin (100 μg/mL), and streptomycin (100 μg/mL). Briefly, L929 cells were seeded in a 96-well plate (20,000 cells per well) and treated with the sterilized PCN-224/KCF-GQDs. After incubation for 24 h, the medium was removed, followed by adding 0.1 mL MTT solution (0.5 mg/mL). The plate was further incubated for another 4 h at 37 ℃ in a humidified environment containing 5% CO2. Then, 0.1 mL of the solution of each well was removed and 0.1 mL DMSO was added. After 10 min incubation in a shaker, 0.1 mL of the solution was transferred to another 96-well plate. The absorbance at 570 nm was recorded by a microplate spectrophotometer (EPOCH2T, USA).

In this research, knitted cotton fabric (KCF, 24 mg/cm2) was selected as the substrate. As shown in Scheme 1 , KCF was first aminated by AEAPMDS (KCF-NH2, 26.05 ± 0.03 mg/cm2), then KCF was coupled with GQDs via amide bond (KCF-GQDs, 27.04 ± 0.04 mg/cm2).Finally, PCN-224 nanoparticles were in-situ grown on the surface of KCF-GQDs (PCN-224/KCF-GQDs, 28.82 ± 0.07 mg/cm2).

We designed an enhanced photodynamic antibacterial knitted cotton fabric via FRET strategy, PCN-224 was in-situ synthesized on the surface of cotton fibers. The morphology of pure PCN-224 nanoparticles was observed by TEM (Fig. 1 panel A), and it was found that the particle size distributed mostly from 60 to 120 nm. The XRD pattern is presented in Fig. 1 panel B, and similar pattern of sharp peaks from 2θ = 4° to 20° confirmed the crystal structure, which indicated the PCN-224 nanoparticles were successfully synthesized [40,43]. The FT-IR spectra of PCN-224 nanoparticles is shown in Fig. 1 panel C, the peaks at 1738 cm−1, 1600 cm−1 and 1415 cm−1 were assigned to CO stretching vibration, CC stretching vibration and C-N bending vibration [39]. The TEM image and HRTEM image of GQDs are presented in Fig. S1. The size of GQDs distributed from 1.0 to 3.7 nm, with an average diameter of 2.44 nm.

Fig. 2 shows the photographic images and SEM images of KCF and PCN-224/KCF-GQDs with different magnifications. Macroscopically, the only difference was that the color of KCF looked white and PCN-224/KCF-GQDs appeared lilac, the reason may be that the size of the PCN-224 was so small (average particle size: 89.99 nm) that they only affect the color of the fabric. After magnification of 50× by SEM (Fig. 2 panel B, E and S2 panel A, C), the knitted loop structure of all the fabrics are clearly presented, a mass of cotton yarn was knitted to cotton fabric which laid the basics for flexible aPDI materials. After solvothermal reaction, distinct morphologies could be seen in Fig. 2 panel C, F and G, aggregative masses of PCN-224 were grown on the surface of cotton fiber with particle-like structure, the average particle size of PCN-224 was 101.15 nm which is 11.16 nm bigger than that of PCN-224 in ethanol. While there was no obvious difference of morphological structure could be observed in KCF-NH2 and KCF-GQDs (Fig. S2 panel B, D), indicating amination and GQDs grafting did not affect cotton fiber obviously. The mechanism of PCN-224 growth on the surface of cotton fiber may include the following two aspects: electrostatic absorption and hydrogen bonding [57,58]. The metal ions in MOFs synthesis chemicals were firstly adsorbed onto the cotton fiber surface by electrostatic adsorption, then the metal ions acted as the reaction sites for other synthesis chemicals, finally resulting in the generated MOFs were attached on cotton fiber surface [59]. In addition, rich hydroxyl groups from the cotton fiber and GQDs may interact with the metal ions or the metal-OH by hydrogen bonding [60,61].

EDS analysis was further used to confirm PCN-224 nanoparticles were grown on the cotton fibers (Fig. 3 ), and the homogenously distributed C, N, O, and, Zr were observed along the cotton fiber, and a notable signal at 2.1 keV is presented in EDS spectra which is corresponding to Zr element, confirming the presence of PCN-224 onto KCF.

The FT-IR spectra in Fig. 4 panel A reveals the chemical groups of KCF, KCF-NH2, KCF-GQDs, and GQDs. The stretching vibration band at approximately 2986–3698 cm−1 showed the presence of the OH groups of cotton cellulose [62,63]. The peaks at 2910 and 2853 cm−1 were attributed to C–H stretching vibrations [64]. The peak of KCF at around 1640 cm−1 was due to the absorbed water bending vibration [65]. Symmetric bending of CH2 was observed at 1427 cm−1. The peaks at 1372, 1316, 1116, and 1159 cm−1 were assigned to OH and CH bending vibrations as well as C–C and C–O stretching vibrations, which confirmed the asymmetric stretching vibration of C–O–C from the glycosidic ring [66]. C–O symmetric stretching was confirmed at the peak of 1056 cm−1, CH out of bending vibration was confirmed at 896 cm−1 and OH out of plane bending is identified at 662 cm−1 [67]. All these FT-IR analysis indicated that the KCF-NH2 and KCF-GQDs still retained the essential structure of cellulose.

However, new peaks of 1582 cm−1 and 1480 cm−1 could be observed in KCF-NH2, which were attributed to N–H bending from NH2 group [68,69]. New peak at 1263 cm−1 could be attributed to the Si–C stretching vibration [70,71]. Strong peak at 1033 cm−1 was due to the Si–O–cellulose stretching vibration, and the peak at 799 as well as 706 cm−1 were assigned to the Si–O–Si stretching vibration [67,72]. The successful grafting of AEAPMDS was supported by the above FT-IR analysis, which laid the foundation for GQDs grafting.

The FT-IR spectra of GQDs have some characteristic bands, where the peaks at 3445 cm−1, 1710 cm−1, 1635 cm−1, 1400 cm−1, 1232 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of O–H, CO, CC, C–H, and C–O, respectively [73]. After grafting reaction, new peaks of 1710 cm−1 and 1232 cm−1 could be observed in KCF-GQDs, which reflected that GQDs had been imported into KCF. The peak at 1650 cm−1 (CO) in the KCF-GQDs system also proved the successful conjugation [74].

XRD was used to confirm the PCN-224 nanoparticles were grown on the surface of cotton fiber successfully. As was shown in Fig. 4 panel B, diffraction peaks of cellulose I crystalline structure could be observed at around 2θ = 14.8°, 16.7°, and 22.8°, which corresponded to (1 −1 0), (1 1 0), and (2 0 0) diffraction planes [75]. While new diffraction peaks at around 2θ = 4.8°, 6.9°, and 8.1° could be observed in PCN-224/KCF-GQDs, indicating PCN-224 nanoparticles was grown onto the KCF successfully. From the analysis of FT-IR and XRD, we concluded that the GQDs were successfully grafted and PCN-224 nanoparticles were successfully introduced.

The UV–visible and PL spectra of GQDs, as well as the UV–visible absorption spectra of PCN-224 were studied (Fig. 5 panel A). For GQDs, there is a typical absorption peak at around 230 nm, which is due to the π-π* transition of CC. The PL maximum emission of the GQDs is achieved at around 430 nm with the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The UV–visible spectra of PCN-224 shows a strong peak at around 425 nm, another four peaks ranging from 500 to 700 nm could be observed, which was attributed to the Q bands from porphyrin ligands inside PCN-224 [76]. The absorption peaks of PCN-224 ensured that the PCN-224 absorption spectra could overlap with the GQDs PL emission spectra, which provided the feasibility for the occurrence of FRET.

The PL spectra of GQDs and PCN-224 aqueous mixing system was explored (Fig. S3). The fluorescence intensity of GQDs ranged from 400 nm to 600 nm, and PCN-224 had a highest emission peak at a wavelength of around 660 nm. As PCN-224 varied from 0 to 1 mg/mL (Fig. S3 panel A), the intensity of GQDs at about 400–600 nm decreased and the intensity of PCN-224 at 660 nm increased gradually. However, as the concentration of PCN-224 remained unchanged and the concentration of GQDs varied from 0 to 6.25 mg/mL, the fluorescence intensity of both GQDs and PCN-224 increased (Fig. S3 panel B). All these demonstrated that the GQDs and PCN-224 could serve as a pair of donor and acceptor in the FRET process.

In order to investigate whether GQDs-PCN-224 donor-acceptor pair can occur on cotton fibers, the PL spectra of KCF-GQDs, PCN-224/KCF-GQDs, and PCN-224/KCF are recorded in Fig. 5 panel B. PCN-224/KCF-GQDs presented two emission peaks under excitation at 360 nm excitation, and the peak position wavelength were in accordance with the PCN-224 and GQDs at 360 nm, respectively. This confirmed the successful grafting of the GQDs and depositing of PCN-224 on the cotton fiber surface. For PCN-224/KCF-GQDs,we further found the PL intensity (wavelength from 400 to 600 nm) was decreased, indicating transferred energy from the GQDs to the PCN-224 through FRET.Therefore, the PCN-224/KCF-GQDs could present a stronger PL intensity at 660 nm, about 4-fold that of PCN-224/KCF.

We explored with DPBF, which is commonly used as a chemical trapping agent for 1O2 detection [77]. The generated 1O2 could react with DPBF specifically, leading to colorless 1,2-dibenzoylbenzene [78]. Fig. 6 reveals the absorbance decrease at around 410 nmwith a function of illumination time, indicating the photooxidation of DPBF by PCN-224/KCF-GQDs and PCN-224/KCF. Furthermore, after 20 min illumination by a laser, the absorbance of DPBF resulted from the PCN-224/KCF-GQDs and PCN-224/KCF decreased 57.94% (linear fitting slope: −0.029) and 36.07% (linear fitting slope: −0.018) of their original values, respectively. The photooxidation rate of DPBF using PCN-224/KCF-GQDs was about 1.61-fold increase than that of PCN-224/KCF, indicating 1O2 generation was greatly increased, which is attributed to the FRET process. The enhanced 1O2 generation is of great importance to photodynamic reaction [79,80]. When the studies were performed in KCF or PCN-224/KCF-GQDs in the absence of illumination, almost no obvious absorbance decrease could be noted (Fig. S4). In summary, these results provided convincing evidence for the 1O2 generation, and the enhanced 1O2 generation was due to the FRET process.

The energy transfer mechanism of 1O2 generation is shown in Fig. 6 panel D. By light of an appropriate wavelength, the PSs absorbs energy to form excited singlet state, then the PSs in the excited singlet state emit fluorescence, or intersystem crossing happens to generate the excited triplet state of PSs. Finally, the PSs in the excited triplet state will give off phosphorescence or react with triplet oxygen (3O2) to generate cytotoxic 1O2 [81].

In vitro aPDI studies of bacteria using PCN-224/KCF-GQDs and PCN-224/KCF were performed by four classes of bacteria, including Gram-negative and Gram-positive ones. The survival rate was presented in Fig. 7 . All the experiments were carried out with a 1 h incubation in the dark followed by 30 min illumination. Fixed illumination intensity was employed, and the initial concentration of bacteria was about 1–5 × 108 CFU/mL which was determined by colony counting.

Both Gram-negative (E. coli, P. aeruginosa) and Gram-positive (S. aureus, B. subtilis) bacterial strains were susceptible to photodynamic inactivation by PCN-224/KCF. With 1 h incubation and 30 min illumination by a Xenonlamp (500 W, 15 cm, λ ≥ 420 nm), the survival rates of E. coli, S. aureus, B. subtilis, and P. aeruginosa were 2.42% (1.62 log units, P = 0.0002), 1.77% (1.80 log units, P < 0.0001), 0.80% (2.10 log units, P < 0.0001), and 5.85% (1.23 log units, P < 0.0005), respectively. It could be noted that there was different inactivation efficiency between Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacterial strains, and the reason was Gram-negative bacteria strains have an extra layer of highly impermeable lipopolysaccharide (LPS), which makes them more resistant to photodynamic inactivation than Gram-positive bacteria [82].

By contrast, the PCN-224/KCF-GQDs exerted a high efficient bactericidal effect, resulting in 99.9999+% elimination (E. coli: 6 log units, P < 0.0002, Fig. S9) (S. aureus: 6 log units, P < 0.0001, Fig. S9) (B. subtilis: 6 log units, P < 0.0001) (P. aeruginosa: 6 log units, P < 0.0005) to the four kinds of bacteria under the same inactivation condition. In other words, there was no CFU could be observed after inactivation. However, no significant bacterial inactivation (<0.4 log unit) could be observed for PCN-224/KCF-GQDs in the absence of illumination and KCF in the presence of illumination, demonstrating the necessity of light illumination and PS. These results confirmed the broad-spectrum photodynamic inactivation of bacteria under reasonable illumination using PCN-224/KCF-GQDs as bactericidal materials. The enhanced photodynamic antibacterial inactivation property was due to the synergistic effect of PCN-224 and GQDs through the FRET process. Overall, by comparison to other studies on PS-loaded materials, PCN-224/KCF-GQDs achieves an equal or higher level of photodynamic inactivation against bacteria (Table S1).

To better interpret the inactivation of bacteria, SEM was used to investigate the morphological change before (Fig. 8 panel A and B) and after illumination (Fig. 8 panel C and D). Prior to illumination, the bacteria displayed an intact and smooth surface with typical size and morphologies of the bacterial cells, certifying no damage to the bacteria: E. coli shaped a rod, S. aureus appears spherical and formed grape-like clusters itself [83]. Whereas, post illumination, serious damage to the cellular structure could be observed, including shrunken/incomplete surface and loss in volume consistent with the leakage of cell membrane. The bacteria after inactivation exhibited obvious changes of cell structure, and these changes were attributed to the oxidative destruction induced by 1O2 through Type II mechanism. The above results were in accordance with some previous observation studies of bacteria destroyed by oxidation including blended fabrics embedded with rose Bengal [84], nanozyme-hydrogel [85], and carbon quantum dots embedded nanofibers [86]. Above all, we attribute these cellular changes to photooxidation caused by 1O2, generated by the photodynamic reaction from the PCN-224 on the surface of cotton fiber. Interestingly, the photooxidation reaction was improved by the FRET process and it has great potential for antimicrobial applications.

In order to conduct the biocompatibility and cytotoxicity of PCN-224/KCF-GQDs, L929 cells were employed to test the in vitro cytotoxicity. Fig. 9 shows the microscopic images and the cell viability, as illustrated, the cell survival rate of L929 cells was over 95% after PCN-224/KCF-GQDs were co-cultured for 24 h. The morphology of cells presented fusiform in Fig. 9 panel B, indicating the cells were in a healthy status. As a whole, the aPDI material, PCN-224/KCF-GQDs, had low cytotoxicity and satisfying biocompatibility, which could be utilized as the self-disinfecting materials to reduce the threat of pathogen transmission.

For the first time, we successfully prepared knitted cotton fabrics with enhanced 1O2 generation which were capable of performing broad-spectrum photodynamic inactivation against both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria. GQDs and PCN-224 nanoparticles were selected as a donor-acceptor FRET pair, and they were immobilized onto the surface of cotton fiber via chemical coupling and in-situ growth. The FRET strategy employed here provided an enhanced 1O2 generation (1.61-fold), which isof great conducivenesstoaPDI. aPDI results demonstrated that PCN-224/KCF-GQDs were capable of achieving 99.9999+% photodynamic inactivation of four kinds of bacterial strains in 30 min, which was an increase of 3.90–4.77 log units than that of PCN-224/KCF. Taken together, these results suggested that GQDs and PCN-224 nanoparticles could be selected as donor-acceptor FRET pair, and this FRET system was successfully introduced to textile materials which could be utilized as high efficient self-disinfecting textiles for pathogen infection prevention.
